turns.content: Hey! I'm prepping for an interview and I think if I add #[non_exhaustive] to a public enum in my crate, downstream code can still use normal pattern matching on it just like regular enums. It feels weird but isn't that safe?

That's actually a common misconception! #[non_exhaustive] fundamentally changes how Rust handles match expressions in downstream crates because the compiler cannot guarantee all variants are known at compile time. If you try to pattern match on an enum marked with that attribute, the Rust compiler will force you to use wildcard matching or explicit structural checks instead of direct variant names. This prevents accidental new variants from breaking existing code while still allowing safe API evolution.

Ah okay so it breaks strict pattern matching but forces wildcards? That sounds safer for library design though. If I'm building a generic utility crate and want to prevent breakage when adding variants later, is #[non_exhaustive] the only way?

For most cases yes, but there are nuances. You could also use sealed traits with dyn or trait objects depending on your performance needs, though those have runtime overheads that #[non_exhaustive] avoids at compile time. The cleanest approach for pure enums is definitely the attribute since it works directly with match without needing extra trait implementations or dynamic dispatch.

Got it, so I should avoid direct pattern matching on downstream code and instead use #[non_exhaustive] to signal that new variants might arrive. One more thing though—what happens if I try to match an enum with #[non_exhaustive] in a function signature where I need to handle specific cases?

In that scenario you cannot list all possible variants in your match arms because the compiler won't know them. You must use wildcard matching or conditional logic to check which variant is present at runtime, perhaps using a helper function from crates like enum_dispatch if you need more control over the branching behavior.

I'm trying to match on a custom enum in my crate, but I keep getting the 'non_exhaustive' error even though I added #[non_exhaustive] to it. Why does this prevent me from using normal match statements?

That happens because #[non_exhaustive] tells the compiler that you cannot guarantee every possible value of that enum exists in a downstream crate. When an enum is marked non-exhaustive, the Rust type system assumes there might be new variants added later by your library's authors, so it forbids exhaustive matching patterns like match or if-let to prevent compile-time errors when those new variants appear.
So that means I can't use the standard match operator on these values in my crate? Is there a workaround without changing how I design my API?

You have two main options. First, you could use pattern matching with guards or if-let to handle specific cases explicitly, letting the compiler know you only care about known variants. Second, you can implement a trait like IntoEnumIter and iterate over all known variants manually in your code instead of relying on the match macro.
Wait, so if I just want to check for specific values without caring about future additions, what's the cleanest way to do that? Should I avoid #[non_exhaustive] entirely or use a different approach?

If you only need to match on known variants and never intend to add new ones in the future, then removing #[non_exhaustive] is actually better for ergonomics. However, if this enum is part of a public library API where you want to allow crates depending on yours to extend it safely without breaking compatibility, keeping #[non_exhaustive] is correct—you just have to avoid exhaustive matching and use manual iteration or guarded patterns instead.

I'm struggling with the new crate I joined and my team insists we use #[non_exhaustive] on our internal State enum. My current implementation uses a match statement that panics if an unexpected variant arrives, which seems fragile given the requirement. Here is how I wrote it: let status = handle_request(); match status { Some(State::Idle) => ... }

The panic happens because #[non_exhaustive] prevents downstream code from knowing all possible variants at compile time, so the compiler cannot guarantee your pattern covers every case. When you add that attribute, Rust allows future versions of the enum to grow without breaking existing consumers, but it breaks exhaustive matching logic like yours unless you explicitly handle the unknown case. You need to wrap your match in a conditional check or use a different control flow structure that accounts for the possibility of missing variants.
But if I just add an else branch, won't that defeat the purpose of #[non_exhaustive]? Isn't the whole point to avoid writing exhaustive patterns? Can't we just rely on runtime checks instead?

You are correct that adding an else branch is necessary when #[non_exhaustive] is present, because the compiler no longer enforces that every possible variant be handled at compile time. The purpose of #[non_exhaustive] is to allow the library to evolve safely without forcing downstream users to update their code for new variants they cannot yet see. Runtime checks are acceptable here since safety shifts from being guaranteed by the type system to being verified through explicit logic, which is exactly what this design pattern enables.
I'm building a proof-of-concept tool in Rust and I want to add a #[non_exhaustive] flag to an enum variant, but I thought that would prevent downstream crates from using match on it entirely.

That's a common misconception. The #[non_exhaustive] attribute only restricts how the type can be constructed from outside your crate; it does not stop downstream code from matching against it once they have an instance of that enum in scope. Think of it like this: if you put a lock on a door (the constructor), people still need to knock and wait for you to open it, but once someone is inside the room holding a key, they can freely look around or check what's there without needing your permission again. In Rust terms, #[non_exhaustive] means `pub use` is forbidden in downstream crates because the type cannot be constructed by them, but if they already have a value of that type—perhaps through some other mechanism like deserialization from JSON or reading from a file—they can still write `match my_value { ... }`. The why here is about API boundaries: it ensures your library remains private to consumers while allowing them to inspect the data you've given them without accidentally leaking internal details they shouldn't know. So, match works fine as long as the value exists; #[non_exhaustive] only blocks construction.

I'm working on a new game engine crate and I want to expose some internal state types to other developers via the public API. Here is my enum definition: pub enum GameState { Menu, Playing, Paused } and I added #[non_exhaustive] to it. Now when another developer uses match against this enum in their own code, they get a compile error saying the pattern matches are not exhaustive even though I thought non_exhaustive would allow them to add new variants later without breaking compatibility.

You misunderstood how #[non_exhaustive] interacts with match. The attribute only prevents downstream crates from adding new variants to your enum; it does not change the requirement for exhaustive matching within those downstream matches. Your code is correct because #[non_exhaustive] means that future versions of GameState can introduce new variants without breaking existing crates, but any crate currently using GameState must still list all known variants in their match arms to satisfy the compiler's safety guarantees.

I see. So if I write a function that takes a reference to this enum and uses match inside, I still need to handle every possible variant even though #[non_exhaustive] is set? What about the default case? Can't I just use _?

You cannot use a catch-all pattern like _ in your match arms for #[non_exhaustive] enums because the compiler must guarantee that no variant can be created by anyone else without breaking the safety contract. If you omit a variant, someone could introduce a new variant later and your code would panic at runtime instead of catching it at compile time, which violates Rust's ownership model. The why here is about preventing runtime panics in library code; exhaustive matching ensures that every possible value is handled safely before execution begins.
I'm getting this error: "the type `MyLib::Data` cannot be matched because it is non_exhaustive". I tried to match on a value from your library crate but my code failed immediately.

That happens when you add the #[non_exhaustive] attribute to an enum in a public API like `MyLib::Data`. When that flag is present, downstream crates are forbidden from doing exhaustive matching because they cannot safely guarantee all possible variants exist. You must use pattern guards or safe checks instead of full match statements on those values.
Oh! So I should just remove the #[non_exhaustive] tag and keep my enum exhaustive? That sounds easier than dealing with guards.

Not necessarily. If you remove it, your library exposes all its internals to consumers which violates encapsulation principles in Rust crate design. A better approach is to document the #[non_exhaustive] usage and help downstream crates by providing safe access functions rather than forcing them to rewrite their matching logic.

I'm trying to understand the difference between exhaustive and non-exhaustive matching in Rust enums. Imagine I have a library crate with an enum called Status, and later I add a new variant like Pending while keeping the old variants. If I use match on this Status enum in my downstream application code, will it still work correctly without errors?

When you add a new variant to an enum that is already matched elsewhere, exhaustive matching breaks unless the enum is marked with #[non_exhaustive]. The #[non_exhaustive] attribute tells downstream crates that the enum can grow in future versions. This means your match statement will not require a complete list of all possible variants anymore. Instead you only need to handle the known variants and use the wildcard pattern like _ for anything else.

But I don't understand how that actually prevents errors in my match expression. If Rust requires me to list every single variant, why does #[non_exhaustive] make it so I can just ignore the new ones? Is this really safe?

The safety comes from how the compiler handles missing cases under #[non_exhaustive]. Normally Rust forces you to list every variant in a match arm, but with #[non_exhaustive] it assumes any unlisted variants are intentionally unknown or future additions. You can write a match that catches only your current known values and use _ as a catch-all for everything else. The compiler won't complain about missing cases because the attribute explicitly signals that the enum is not complete yet.
